EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and dry conditions which started in July, persisted into August with dry lightning events triggering numerous wild fires in north central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry lightning triggered numerous wild fires in Idaho County in August. 115,937 acres burned. The Boundary Junction Fire near the Clearwater County border north of Highway 12, destroyed one Out Building with 4671 acres burned. The largest wild fire, the Rattlesnake near Dixie burned 76,112 acres with three Out Buildings destoyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the north and central Idaho Panhandle, as well as the Coeur D'Alene area.  Valley snow accumulations from this event include 8 inches three miles west of Naples, 6 inches in Sandpoint, 4 inches in Athol and Coeur D'Alene, and 5 inches in Kellogg.  In the Central Panhandle Mountains, Silver Mountain received 14 inches of accumulation, and Lookout Pass 12 inches.
Three-quarter inch hail fell in Challis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three-quarter inch hail reported in Challis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three-quarter inch hail fell in Challis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of over 1 inch in less than one hour caused three mudslides along highway 75 in Challis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began on July 4th about 10 miles northwest of St Anthony.  It was a grass and brush fire that burned about 1100 acres.  The Red Road north of the Sand Hills resort between Fremont and clark County was closed due to the fire on the 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began on July 4th about 10 miles northwest of St Anthony.  It was a grass and brush fire that burned about 1100 acres.  The Red Road north of the Sand Hills resort between Fremont and Clark County was closed due to the fire on the 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 580 acre wildfire was started by lightning on the 8th.  The grass and brush fire was just to the north of Preston came within a quarter mile of homes on the afternoon of the 9th but no homes were evacuated.  The Red Point and Maple Grove campgrounds were closed on the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 580 acre wildfire was started by lightning on the 8th.  The grass and brush fire was just to the north of Preston and came within a quarter mile of homes on the afternoon of the 9th but no homes were evacuated.  the Red Point and Maple Grove campgrounds were closed on the 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began July 4th southeast of Malta and eventually burned 73,000 acres of grass, brush and juniper and threatened homes and farms in the area.  The fire was considered contained on July 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began July 4th southeast of Malta and eventually burned 73,000 acres of grass, brush and juniper and threatened homes and farms in the area.  The fire was considered contained on July 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This fire was started by lightning on August 1st 18 miles northwest of Challis and consumed nearly 150,000 acres.  Eventually this complex contained 3 separate fires in Custer and Lemhi Counties and was considered 100 percent contained by the end of September.  It destroyed 4 outbuildings in August with no further damage in September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This fire was started by lightning on August 1st 18 miles northwest of Challis and consumed nearly 150,000 acres.  Eventually this complex contained 3 separate fires in Custer and Lemhi Counties and was considered 100 percent contained by the end of September.  It destroyed 4 outbuildings in August with no further damage in September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely heavy and wet snow fell in the Snake River Plain.  5 to 7 inches of snow fell in Idaho Falls, Rigby and the benches of Pocatello with 3 to 5 inches in the cities of Rexburg, Blackfoot, Chubbuck and  Pocatello.  Extensive tree damage occurred due to the weight of the snow and the fact that it was so early in the season, many trees were still fully leaved.  Many power lines in Idaho Falls were snapped by falling tree branches.  Extensive damage from downed trees occurred in the Mountain View Cemetary in Pocatello.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very heavy 6 inch wet snow fell in Idaho Falls.  Several tree and branch damage occurred with many tree branches snapping power lines as they fell.  5 inches fell in Rigby with 4 inches in Rexburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early December storm brought amounts as high as one to two feet in portions of the Central Mountains and Upper Snake Highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thirteen inches of snow was reported at Mill Creek Summit with ten inches at Smiley Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front pushed south through southeast Idaho on January 11th allowing bitterly cold temperatures along with strong northerly winds to take over.  By 1 am on the 12th, several locations dropped below -20 wind chill temperature with readings on the morning of the 12th as cold as -35 in some areas.  The Blackfoot, Snake River and Firth school districts all closed due to the cold on January 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures reached -20 to -35 below after midnight on the 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong cross winds caused Highway 20 between Idaho Falls and the Idaho National Laboratory to be shut down from 6 pm to 7 pm MDT on August 16th.  The poor visibility caused multiple accidents that blocked the highway causing the closure.  There were 3 separate crashes at mile marker 275. Three people were transported to the Eastern Idaho Regional Medical Center with minor injuries.

Three people were transported to the Eastern Idaho Regional Medical Center with minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A catalytic converter ignited a large tall grass fire near the Utah border near Stone Idaho burnign 11,400 acres of federal, state and private lands.  The fire was started on June 20th and considered contained by June 24th.  No structures or buildings were damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A catalytic coverter from an automobile ignited an 11,400 acre wildfire near the Utah border near Stone, Idaho.  The fire began on June 20th and was considered contained by June 24th.  No buildings or structures were lost in the tall grass fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Howard wildfire begain on June 17th in the hills just to the southwest of the city of Pocatello.  The 1,650 acre grass and brush fire was considered contained on June 21st.  No buildings or sturctures were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two fires combined about 12 miles northeast of Preston and became known as the Cleveland fire.  The Cleveland fire started on August 10th and was considered contained on August 24th.  It burned 16,269 acres.  Highway 34 was closed between Preston and Grace on August 12th due to the fire. The fire destroyed 10 outbuildings and damaged power lines but no evacuations were ordered.

CZ_NAME: BIG AND LITTLE WOOD RIVER REGION
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant timber, brush and grass fire began on August 16th 8 miles to the west of the city of Ketchum due to a lightning strike.  The fire exhibited extreme behavior and in a week had grown to over 40,000 acres.  Evacuations were ordered in the vicinity of the fire in Blaine County beginning on August 19th and continued through the end of August.  Over 7,000 residences, 150 outbuildings and 340 commercial structures were considered threatened by the fire.  No structures were destroyed.  By September 5th the wildfire had consumed 48,520 acres and was considered 100 percent contained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire that began in August west of Ketchum was finally considered 100 percent contained on September 5th after consuming 48,250 acres.  Over 7,000 residences, 150 outbuildings and 340 commerical structures were considererd threatened by the fire.  No structures were destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 19 and 20, a strong disturbance moved east across North Central Idaho. Snowfall amounts were enhanced by a strong and moist westerly flow that was already entrenched over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Idaho road department reported 7 to 8 inches of new snowfall accumulations at Lost Trail Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 29 and 30, moist northwesterly flow persisted over the northern Rockies. During this period, a series of shortwave disturbances moved through and produced heavy snow accumulations and strong winds across North Central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Idaho road department reported 8 inches of new snow at Lost Trail Pass at 0930 am MST on December 30. Snowfall totals for this event ranged from 7.5 inches at Gibbonsville to 16 inches at Lost Trail Pass.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather observation sites between Elk River and Elk Butte reported between 6 and 11.5 inches of new snowfall accumulations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On November 26 and 27, a vigorous winter storm moved east across North Central Idaho and produced heavy snowfall amounts across the higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather observation sites at Lolo Pass and at the Powell Ranger Station reported 9 and 11 inches, respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 15 and 16, a winter storm brought heavy snow amounts of 8 to 9 inches along with gusty west to northwest winds to Clearwater County Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Elk Butte and Shanghai Summit Snotel sites received 8 inches new snow. Hemlocke Butte received 9 inches new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 19 and 20, a strong winter storm brought very heavy snow and strong gusty winds to the Clearwater Mountains of north central Idaho. Total snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 7 inches in the valleys to near two feet in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 10 inches in the valleys and 15 to 25 inches in the mountains. Hemlocke Butte recorded 25 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 19 and 20, a strong winter storm brought very heavy snow and strong gusty winds to the Clearwater Mountains of north central Idaho. Total snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 7 inches in the valleys to near two feet in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 8 inches in the valleys and 9 to 12 inches in the mountains. Lolo Pass recorded 12 inches of new snow. Wind gusts of 30 to 44 mph were recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 29, a strong, negatively tilted shortwave trough moved to the northeast across the region. An intense mid level jet streak moved around the base of the trough and through north central Idaho during the early evening hours. An intense, tornadic storm moved across the area producing widespread damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager, USFS personnel and storm spotters reported numerous large trees and limbs blown down (intermittantly) along the storm track.

An intense mid level jet streak moved around the base of the trough and through north central Idaho during the early evening hours. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm moist flow ahead of a slow moving cold front fed a broad precipitation field including embedded convection over Idaho. 24 hour rainfall amounts were generally an inch to an inch and one half with locally heavier amounts of 2 inches. Some locations received over an inch of rain during a 9 hour period and over a third of an inch in one hour. Mountain locations above 8000 feet received 4 to 8 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy Rain Accumlations Estimated Near 1.5 inches with 0.38 Inches Occuring Between 2 AM and 3 AM on Portions of the Cascade Complex Burn Area Caused a Debris Flow with Water, Mud, and Rocks Flowing on to    Forest Service Road 579 about 4 Miles North of Deadwood Lodge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 3, a Pacific storm system brought 7 to 15 inches of new snowfall accumulations to the mountains of North Central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches new snow were reported to have fallen at Lolo and Savage Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On April 17 an 18, an upper level trough moved across North Central Idaho producing heavy snowfall in the higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven to eight inches of heavy snow was received at the Savage Pass and Mountain Meadows Snotel sites during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 5 and 6, a strong winter storm brought heavy snow and local gusty winds to the Clearwater Mountains of North Central Idaho. Visibility was reduced in blowing snow over mountain passes. Total snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 8 inches in the lower elevations, with 14 to 24 of new snow in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative Observer at Headquarters reported 8 inches new snow with 9 inches at Pierce. Snotel data reported 22 to 28 inches above 5000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 20, a strong short wave trough and surface front moved across the area creating a line of strong to severe thunderstorms that produced hail up to the size of golf balls over portions of West Central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person reported hail the size of golf balls at Slate Creek along with strong winds and heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex upper level system moving in from Northern California on the 25th spread instability across parts of Idaho resulting in convection with moderate to heavy snow and thunderstorms in the West Central and Boise mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to seven inches of snow fell in the West Central Mountains Zone from 5 AM to 5 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful spring storm moved through the Northern Great Basin spreading wet snow and gusty winds across Southern Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven inches of snow fell at South Mountain RAWS by 4 AM March 28, 2007. The significant snow accumulations were generally above 5800 feet in the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful spring storm moved through the Northern Great Basin spreading wet snow and gusty winds across Southern Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fourteen to Fifteen inches of wet snow fell near Rogerson and gusts up to 49 MPH in the Southern Twin Falls Zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture moved into the North Idaho Panhandle ahead of a deep low pressure system off the coast, bringing heavy snow to the North Idaho Panhandle, mainly close to the Canadian border.  Eastport picked up six inches of snow.
A series of waves and impulses moving east off the Pacific Ocean brought heavy snow across the Northern and Central Idaho Panhandle mountains.  Accumulations mostly ranged from 7 to 14 inches.
A series of waves and impulses moving east off the Pacific Ocean brought heavy snow across the Northern and Central Idaho Panhandle mountains.  Accumulations mostly ranged from 7 to 14 inches.
A pacific weather system tracked through the inland northwest, bringing heavy snow accumulations to the North and Central Idaho Panhandle Mountains.  Snow accumulations were generally near 8 inches.
A pacific weather system tracked through the inland northwest, bringing heavy snow accumulations to the North and Central Idaho Panhandle Mountains.  Snow accumulations were generally near 8 inches.

A fast moving pacific system brought heavy snow to the Coeur D'Alene area Christmas evening, followed by numerous snow showers into the morning of the 26th.  Snow accumulations include six inches in Coeur D'Alene and 4.5 inches in Rathdrum.

A moist pacific weather system tracked through the inland northwest where heavy snow fell in the Central Idaho Panhandle.  Snow accumulations include eight to ten inches in the mountains, with Kellogg also picking up four inches.

A weather system dropping down from Canada moved through North Idaho resulting in heavy snow accumulations on the palouse and Camas Prairie.  The Moscow and Winchester areas received four to five inches of snow.  A pair of accidents on the Idaho Palouse were partially contributed from snow and ice on roadways, which resulted in two indirect injuries.  Several other small fender-bender accidents occurred in Moscow.  In Lewiston, two inches of snow fell which contributed to a major accident on the Spalding Bridge east of Lewiston on U.S. Highway 95.  This accident stopped traffic for 3.5 hours.

A strong Pacific winter storm coming onshore in Oregon spread six to twelve inches of snow in the mountains of Idaho and Southeast Oregon between December 2nd and 3rd. Locally heavy amounts of fifteen inches were reported at some sites.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative observers and trained spotters reported amounts ranging from 7 inches near Yellowpine, Idaho to 12 inches near Cascade and McCall. The Long Valley SNOTEL located four miles north of Donnelly recorded nine inches. The elevation at the site is 4,890 feet.

A moist pacific system tracked through north Idaho bringing heavy snow.  The highest accumulations occurred in the Central Panhandle Mountains where seven to 12 inches accumulated.  In the North Idaho Panhandle, accumulations in the valleys ranged from four to six inches.

A strong winter storm brought heavy snow to much of northern Idaho.  A cold arctic air mass that moved into the area at the end of November was replaced by a moist southwest flow coming up from Hawaii.  This resulted in a prolonged period of snowfall as warm moist air overran cold air at the surface.  The heaviest accumulations from this event occurred in cities closer to the Canadian Border where valley accumulations ranged anywhere 17-25 inches from Sandpoint to Bonners Ferry.  Further south Athol picked up six inches.  In the Coeur D'Alene area and Idaho Palouse accumulations ranged from three to six inches.  In the Central Panhandle Mountains valley accumulations also ranged from three to six inches, but 12 to 16 inches of snow accumulated at ski resorts.

On November 12, a strong cold front moved across North Central Idaho and produced high winds across the Camas Prairie near Grangeville and the Salmon River Region north of Riggins, as upper level winds mixed down to the surface and interracted with strong pressure gradient winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong pressure gradient and upper level driven winds produced sustained winds of around 43 mph with measured gusts as high as 81 mph. Damage to several buildings in Grangeville was reported including a roof of a school, furniture store and the support of a drive-in theatre screen. Numerous trees were also blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On November 12, a strong cold front moved across North Central Idaho and produced high winds across the Camas Prairie near Grangeville and the Salmon River Region north of Riggins, as upper level winds mixed down to the surface and interracted with strong pressure gradient winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong pressure gradient and upper level driven winds produced sustained winds of around 45 mph with estimate gusts to near 60 mph. Storm spotters reported roof damage to homes and fences down in Riggins.|County emergency management reported that 3 semi trucks were blown over on Highway 95. There were two injuries.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter in Weippe reported that between 8 and 11 inches of new snowfall had fallen across town. Snotel observations also reported snowfall amounts as high up to 11 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The weather observation site at Lolo Pass reported 14 inches of new snowfall. Weather spotters in Elk City and Red River Hot Springs reported between 6 and 8 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist theta-E ridge, a developing 700 mb low, and an upper level jet speed maximum lifted a negatively tilted trough through South Central Idaho early on the 27th producing heavy snow showers in Twin Falls County.

A small but strong short wave brought heavy snow across portions of the Idaho Palouse.  Temperature initially were above freezing resulting in initial melting on roads...and then compact snow over ice as temperatures dropped below freezing.  This contributed to several non-serious traffic accidents.  Snow amounts from this event include 8 inches 4 miles NNE of Moscow, and 5 inches 4 miles ENE of Tekoa.

High winds occurred across portions of the Coeur D'Alene area and near Deary near the Central Panhandle Mountains.  A wind gust of 67 MPH was measured at Pleasant View.  Several trees were knocked down by the winds, with 2,150 people losing power.  In Coeur D'Alene, a tree fell on a 2003 Toyota Land Rover.  A strong wind gust near Deary led to roof damage.  The property damage from this event is estimated at $4,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A two-hundred and ninety acre wildfire just north of Ketchum in the Trail Creek area in Blaine County was contained with no homes or structures lost.  The cost to contain the fire was set at $680,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began in the Trail Creek area of Blaine County just to the north of Ketchum on Friday June 22nd.  The fire was 290 acres and burned sagebrush, grass and cottonwood trees.  No homes or structures were damaged and the fire was considered contained on June 25th.  Cost to contain the fire was estimated at $680,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely heavy and wet snow fell in the Snake River Plain.  5 to 7 inches of snow fell in Idaho Falls, Rigby and the benches of Pocatello with 3 to 5 inches in the cities of Rexburg, Blackfoot, Chubbuck and  Pocatello.  Extensive tree damage occurred due to the weight of the snow and the fact that it was so early in the season, many trees were still fully leaved.  Many power lines in Idaho Falls were snapped by falling tree branches.  Extensive damage from downed trees occurred in the Mountain View Cemetary in Pocatello.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to ten inches of heavy wet snow fell on the west and each benches of Pocatello with 3 to 5 inches in the cities of Chubbuck and Pocatello.  The weight of the snow caused many trees and branches to be snapped and downed.  The Mountain View Cemetary took extensive damage during the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm brought heavy snow to extreme southeastern Idaho with 12 inches at the Slug Creek Divide SNOTEL 14 miles north of Montpelier.  10 inches fell at the Pine Creek Pass SNOTEL east of Idaho Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve inches of snow fell at the Slug Creek Divide SNOTEL site 14 miles north of Montpelier.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 2 and 3, a potent winter storm moved across North Central Idaho. Blizzard conditions were observed on the Camas Prairie as strong southerly winds blew the falling and drifting snow around to create white-out conditions. Elsewhere, high winds were also observed near Slate Creek. The Weippe Prairie and Clearwater Mountains experienced a winter storm as heavy snow fell and as winds increased throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were blown down by wind in the Grangeville area. Wind gusts to 59 miles per hour were reported at the Grangeville airport between 6:30 pm and 7:30 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 2 and 3, a potent winter storm moved across North Central Idaho. Blizzard conditions were observed on the Camas Prairie as strong southerly winds blew the falling and drifting snow around to create white-out conditions. Elsewhere, high winds were also observed near Slate Creek. The Weippe Prairie and Clearwater Mountains experienced a winter storm as heavy snow fell and as winds increased throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mount Idaho weather observation site reported a wind gust to 66 mph at 7:03 am MST. Minor roof damage was reported in Grangeville and a few large trees were reported to have been blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 29 and 30, moist northwesterly flow persisted over the northern Rockies. During this period, a series of shortwave disturbances moved through and produced heavy snow accumulations and strong winds across North Central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported between 8 and 15 inches of new snowfall accumulations in Pierce, Weippe, Headquarters and Cardiff. In addition, wind gusts to 27 mph were observed at nearby weather observation stations. Spotters near Weippe also reported blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 29 and 30, moist northwesterly flow persisted over the northern Rockies. During this period, a series of shortwave disturbances moved through and produced heavy snow accumulations and strong winds across North Central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported between 7 and 18 inches of new snowfall accumulations in Elk City, Dixie, and Lolo Pass. In addition, wind gusts to 40 mph were observed at a nearby weather observation station at Lodgepole. Blowing and drifting snow was reported by spotters in Elk City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 19 and 20, a strong disturbance moved east across North Central Idaho. Snowfall amounts were enhanced by a strong and moist westerly flow that was already entrenched over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 8 and 9.5 inches of new snow fell at the Shanghai Summit and Elk Butte weather observation sites respectively in the Northern Clearwater Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 19 and 20, a strong disturbance moved east across North Central Idaho. Snowfall amounts were enhanced by a strong and moist westerly flow that was already entrenched over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lolo Pass received 13 inches, Dixie 12 inches and Mount Idaho 10 inches of new snowfall.

Blizzard conditions were observed on the Camas Prairie as strong southerly winds blew the falling and drifting snow around to create white-out conditions. Elsewhere, high winds were also observed near Slate Creek. The Weippe Prairie and Clearwater Mountains experienced a winter storm as heavy snow fell and as winds increased throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds at Slate Creek gusted between 60 and 77 mph betweem 7:00 pm and 12:32 am. Minor roof damage was observed in Slate Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 2 and 3, a potent winter storm moved across North Central Idaho. Blizzard conditions were observed on the Camas Prairie as strong southerly winds blew the falling and drifting snow around to create white-out conditions. Elsewhere, high winds were also observed near Slate Creek. The Weippe Prairie and Clearwater Mountains experienced a winter storm as heavy snow fell and as winds increased throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Idaho County emergency management reported blizzard conditions across the Camas Prairie. The white-out conditions caused several car accidents. One snow plow slid off the road. Several 3 to 4 foot snow drifts were reported on area roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 2 and 3, a potent winter storm moved across North Central Idaho. Blizzard conditions were observed on the Camas Prairie as strong southerly winds blew the falling and drifting snow around to create white-out conditions. Elsewhere, high winds were also observed near Slate Creek. The Weippe Prairie and Clearwater Mountains experienced a winter storm as heavy snow fell and as winds increased throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Headquarters received 7.5 inches and Shanghai Summit received 17 inches of new snowfall accumulations during this event. Significant blowing and drifting snow was reported near Weippe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second of a pair of powerful storms hit the mountains of Southwest Idaho leaving the area with another seven to twelve inches of snow in addition to the heavy snow from the previous days event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative observers in McCall and Cascade reported six to seven inches of new snow during the day and a trained spotter near Cuprum reported nine inches. Some of the SNOTEL sites reported seven to twelve inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 2 and 3, a potent winter storm moved across North Central Idaho. Blizzard conditions were observed on the Camas Prairie as strong southerly winds blew the falling and drifting snow around to create white-out conditions. Elsewhere, high winds were also observed near Slate Creek. The Weippe Prairie and Clearwater Mountains experienced a winter storm as heavy snow fell and as winds increased throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lolo Pass received 20 inches of snow from this event. Dixie received 13 inches from this event. The Lodgepole and Red River weather observation sites reported wind gusts to 29 miles per hour and 30 miles per hour, respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second of a pair of powerful storms hit the mountains of Southwest Idaho leaving the area with another seven to twelve inches of snow in addition to the heavy snow from the previous days event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of public and cooperative observer reports put around six inches of new snow in the Idaho City area and six to eight inches near Placerville and Centerville. SNOTEL sites recorded seven to twelve inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second of a pair of powerful storms hit the mountains of Southwest Idaho leaving the area with another seven to twelve inches of snow in addition to the heavy snow from the previous days event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer at Fairfield reported six inches of new snow and a spotter near Blaine reported seven inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large Pacific winter storm belted the mountains of Southwest Idaho with a one-two punch during a period from the 18th through the 20th of December. The first of the two storms put down six to ten inches in the mountain valleys and ten to twenty inches in the higher elevations mainly above 6000 feet. There was a short break in the evening of the 18th before the next storm hit the area early the next day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bogus Basin Ski Resort reported eleven inches of new snow on their website during the dayimte hours and a trained spotter in Pine, Idaho reported 10 inches in the evening. Some of the high mountain SNOTEL sites reported 10 to 15 inches during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large Pacific winter storm belted the mountains of Southwest Idaho with a one-two punch during a period from the 18th through the 20th of December. The first of the two storms put down six to ten inches in the mountain valleys and ten to twenty inches in the higher elevations mainly above 6000 feet. There was a short break in the evening of the 18th before the next storm hit the area early the next day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports from cooperative observers and trained spotters indicated six to twelve inches of new snow fell during the day. SNOTEL sites recorded around ten to eleven inches for most locations and twenty inches at Deadwood Summit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Coeur D'Alene River at Cataldo rose above flood stage, which resulted in minor flooding.  The river crested at 44.3 feet near 9:30 AM PST, above the flood stage of 43.0 feet.  CCC Road was closed and the Cataldo Campground received minor flooding.  The Old Mission Landing boat dock at Old Mission State Park was also under water.  The majority of the flooding in Kootenai county was to fields and agricultural land.  In Shoshone county, Thomson Pass Road at milepost 4 was flooded, causing the road to be closed for two days.

A 26 year old snowmobiler was killed in an avalanche about 15 miles west of Jackson near Palisades Peak.  The victim was buried under 8 or 9 feet of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 26 year old snowmobiler was killed in an avalanche about 15 miles west of Jackson near Palisades Peak.  The victim was buried under 8 or 9 feet of snow and could not be resuscitated by companions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interstate 15 was closed from Idaho Falls to Roberts from mile post 119 and 134 from 3:45 pm until 9:10 pm.  Visibility was frequently reduced to zero miles with several accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 40 mph were recorded in Roberts from 4:30 pm until 6:00 pm MDT and extensive blowing dust reduced visibility to near zero miles on interstate 15 and it was closed between mile posts 119 and 134 due to the winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tracked from Mullan north into Murray.  In Mullan, hail up to 1.75 inches in diameter was observed, with 0.88 inch diameter hail observed in Murray.

A line of severe thunderstorms tracked through north Idaho causing extensive damage from Kootenai county north to the United States and Canadian border.  Numerous trees were blown down with power outages common due to winds between 60-70 MPH.  Over 100 power poles had to be replaced, including at least 70 that snapped near Priest Lake.  At least 15,000 homes in Kootenai, Bonner, and Boundary counties lost power. Every home in Boundary county lost power.  Trees fell onto several homes, cars, and roadways, including highway 95 which was blocked north of Bonners Ferry and between Bonners Ferry and Sandpoint. The Army National Guard responded to provide additional aid to emergency personnel.  The port of entry into Canada briefly closed due to the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Shower Bath fire was started by lightning on August 1st 18 miles northwest of Challis on the Custer County and Lemhi County border and continued unabated through the month of August.  It spread to 130,784 acres by the 31st and was still considered zero percent contained.  By the 31st the Shower Bath complex contained 3 separate fires including the Shower Bath and Red Bluff fires in Custer County with one in Lemhi County.  Only four outbuildings were considered destroyed in the month.

It spread to 130,784 acres by the 31st and was still considered zero percent contained. By the 31st the Shower Bath complex contained 3 separate fires including the Shower Bath and Red Bluff fires in Custer County with one in Lemhi County. Only four outbuildings were considered destroyed in the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm caused power lines to come down 2 miles north of Mackay closed roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines downed by thunderstorm winds just north of Mackay closing roads.

CZ_NAME: BIG AND LITTLE WOOD RIVER REGION
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant timber, brush and grass fire began on August 16th 8 miles to the west of the city of Ketchum due to a lightning strike and had grown to 400 acres by August 18th.  The fire exhibited extreme behavior and in a week had grown to over 40,000 acres.  Evacuations were ordered in the vicinity of the fire in Blaine County beginning on August 19th and continued through the end of the month.  Over 7,000 primary residences, 150 outbuildings and 340 commercial structures were considered threatened by the fire.  No structures were destroyed.  By August 31st the wildfire had consumed 46,231 acres and was considered 66 percent contained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant timber, brush and grass fire began on August 16th 8 miles to the west of the city of Ketchum from a lightning strike and had grown to 400 acres by August 18th. The fire exhibited extreme behavior and in a week had grown to over 40,000 acres. Evacuations were ordered in the vicinity of the fire in Blaine County beginning on August 19th and continued through the end of the month. Over 7,000 primary residences, 150 outbuildings and 340 commercial structures were considered threatened by the fire. No structures were destroyed. By August 31st the wildfire had consumed 46,231 acres and was considered 66 percent contained.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense 50 to 60 mph thunderstorm winds caused a small wildfire of 1000 acres to grow and spread rapidly to 45,862 acres just south and southeast of Shoshone.  No homes were damaged, but 5 outbuildings were destroyed and 5 were damaged.  Damage was caused to two large power transmission lines maintained by Idaho Power.

A 27 foot sailboat sank near Blackwell Island in Coeur D'Alene after lightning struck the sailboat.  An electrical charge from the lightning also traveled up power lines to a home and blew out two TV's and damaged several electrical sockets.  Other homes and businesses received minor damage.  Lightning struck a business putting a hole in a concrete wall.  About 2,000 power outages resulted from the storm.

The Coyote Creek Fire, centered nine miles east of Lewiston, was a human caused fire that burned over 1,000 acres.  The fire destroyed one home, severely burned a power pole, and briefly closed a stretch of highway 12 near Spalding.

A tornado touched down just north of Soda Springs over the Monsanto Plant.  The tornado was on the ground for about 7 miles and 10 minutes.  Numerous 12 to 14 inch diameter trees were snapped off but no buildings were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The airmass over the Intermountain Region rapidly destabilized as a cold front originating from the Pacific Northwest set off a round of severe convection over parts of Southwest Idaho.
Three-quarter inch hail reported by weather spotter in Rapid Creek area near Inkom.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter reported three-quarter inch hail in Rapid Creek northeast of Inkom.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough lifting through Central Oregon and the associated jet stream speed max over Southwest Idaho lead to the development of severe thunderstorms over the Nampa and Caldwell, Idaho areas.
